records.ety: [OE. glotten, fr. OF. glotir, gloutir, L. glutire, gluttire; cf. Gr. <?/ to eat, Skr. gar. Cf. Gluttion, Englut.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Glutted; p. pr. & vb. n. Glutting.]

defs: To swallow, or to swallow greedlly; to gorge.
quotes: Though every drop of water swear against it, And gape at widest to glut him.

defs: To fill to satiety; to satisfy fully the desire or craving of; to satiate; to sate; to cloy.
quotes: His faithful heart, a bloody sacrifice, Torn from his breast, to glut the tyrant's eyes.
The realms of nature and of art were ransacked to glut the wonder, lust, and ferocity of a degraded populace.

defs: To eat gluttonously or to satiety.
quotes: Like three horses that have broken fence, And glutted all night long breast-deep in corn.

defs: Plenty, to satiety or repletion; a full supply; hence, often, a supply beyond sufficiency or to loathing; over abundance; as, a glut of the market.
quotes: A glut of those talents which raise men to eminence.

defs: Something that fills up an opening; a clog.
A wooden wedge used in splitting blocks.
A piece of wood used to fill up behind cribbing or tubbing.
A bat, or small piece of brick, used to fill out a course.
An arched opening to the ashpit of a kiln.
A block used for a fulcrum.
The broad-nosed eel (Anguilla latirostris), found in Europe, Asia, the West Indies, etc.
